🌍 Year 9 — World War One & The League of Nations

Nelson and Trafalgar, World War One and M.A.I.N. causes, and the League of Nations.

📘 T&L: Critical Thinking & Collaboration⭐ HPL: Analysing · Hard Working · Realising🌍 SDG 12–15: Global Stewardship☮️ SDG 16: Peace & Justice🤖 AI: G8 Solution Builder

⚓ Britain at War — The Naval Context

While Napoleon's armies swept across Europe, Britain's most powerful weapon was its navy. The strategy was simple: use the Royal Navy to protect merchant ships, seize French colonies, and prevent any invasion fleet from crossing the Channel. The French — obsessed with their revolutionary ideals and land campaigns — largely ignored the naval war. This gave Britain a critical advantage at sea.

⛵ Life at Sea — The Wooden World

A warship like HMS Victory was home to up to 800 men and boys for months at a time. Life was brutal, disciplined, and extraordinarily dangerous — even before any battle began.

⚓ Daily Life Aboard Ship
  • 🛏️ Sailors lived in cramped spaces between the great guns
  • 🍖 Food: salt beef, hard biscuits (often full of weevils)
  • 🍺 Grog (watered-down rum) was the daily drink
  • 🔪 Flogging with a cat-o'-nine-tails for disobedience
  • ⛓️ Impressment: men forcibly seized from streets and ships
⚠️ The Dangers
  • 🌊 Storms and shipwrecks killed more sailors than enemy action
  • 💀 Disease: typhus, scurvy, dysentery ravaged entire crews
  • 🔥 Fire was catastrophic on a wooden ship full of gunpowder
  • 💣 Flying splinters caused as many casualties as cannonballs
  • 🩸 Surgeons amputated limbs below deck without anaesthetic
📜 Samuel Leech, sailor, describing life aboard a warship
"A ship containing a set of human machinery, in which every man is a wheel, a band, or a crank, moving... to the will of its machinist — the all-powerful captain."

Apply WACA: who was Leech? When did he write this? What was his purpose in writing?

⚔️ Nelson's Career — From Boy Sailor to National Hero

Horatio Nelson went to sea aged just 12. By his death at 47, he had become Britain's most celebrated commander — inspiring extraordinary loyalty through personal bravery, tactical genius, and genuine care for his men.

1771Born in Norfolk, son of a clergyman. Joined the Royal Navy aged 12.
1793Given command of HMS Agamemnon — his first major independent command.
1797Battle of St Vincent: personally boarded two Spanish ships. Lost his right arm at Tenerife shortly after.
1798Battle of the Nile: destroyed Napoleon's fleet in Egypt, stranding his army. National hero overnight.
1801Battle of Copenhagen: famously raised his telescope to his blind eye to ignore the order to retreat — and won.
1805Battle of Trafalgar, 21 October. Britain's greatest naval victory — and Nelson's death.

🧠 The Nelson Touch

What made Nelson truly exceptional was not just his bravery — it was his leadership philosophy. Before Trafalgar, Nelson gathered all his captains together and explained his entire battle plan. This was revolutionary. Other admirals gave orders and expected blind obedience. Nelson gave his captains understanding — and trusted them to use their own judgement in the heat of battle.

🤝
Share the Plan
Before Trafalgar, Nelson held a dinner explaining the entire battle plan. Every captain knew what to do — even if Nelson was killed.
⚡
Trust Initiative
"No captain can do very wrong if he places his ship alongside that of the enemy." He trusted them to think independently.
💝
Care for Men
Nelson shared prize money fairly, visited the sick, remembered men's names. His sailors called themselves his 'band of brothers.'
🎲
Calculated Risk
His two-column plan at Trafalgar broke every rule of conventional naval warfare — but it worked.
📜 Nelson to his captains, eve of Trafalgar, October 1805
"No captain can do very wrong if he places his ship alongside that of the enemy."

What does this reveal about Nelson's leadership philosophy? How did this differ from other naval commanders?

🌊 The Battle of Trafalgar — 21 October 1805

Napoleon needed control of the English Channel to invade Britain. The combined French and Spanish fleet of 33 ships faced Nelson's 27. Nelson's plan: divide his fleet into two columns and drive straight through the enemy line, breaking it into three sections that could then be overwhelmed separately.

⚔️ The Battle — Step by Step
11:45amNelson signals: "England expects that every man will do his duty"
NoonTwo British columns break the Franco-Spanish line
1:15pmNelson shot by a French sniper on HMS Redoutable
4:30pmNelson dies. Final words: "Thank God I have done my duty"
End21 enemy ships captured or sunk. No British ships lost.
🏆 Why Trafalgar Mattered
  • 🛡️ Napoleon's invasion of Britain became impossible
  • 🌊 Britain ruled the seas unchallenged for the next century
  • 💰 Trade routes were safe — Britain's economy boomed
  • 🏴 Nelson became the greatest British hero of his age
  • ⚓ HMS Victory is preserved at Portsmouth today
🎯 Activity 1: Battle Guide

Create a Battle Guide for the Battle of Trafalgar. Include:

  • An introduction explaining the naval context and why Britain needed to win
  • Key people: Nelson, Collingwood, Villeneuve — one paragraph each
  • A diagram showing Nelson's two-column attack plan
  • Step-by-step account of the battle with key timings
  • The aftermath: why Trafalgar changed the course of history
🎯 Activity 2: The Nelson Touch
🎯 Activity 3: Life at Sea Investigation
  • You are a sailor on HMS Victory in 1805. Write a diary entry describing: your daily routine, the food and conditions, impressment, and your feelings before the Battle of Trafalgar
  • Apply WACA to Source A (Samuel Leech). What are its strengths and limitations as evidence of life at sea?
  • 💎 Research: find another primary source about life in the Royal Navy in this period. Apply WACA to it and compare it with Leech's account.
🎓 Independent Study: Nelson — Hero or Flawed Man?
🥈 SilverExplain what the 'Nelson Touch' was and why it made Nelson's fleet so effective.
🥇 Gold'Nelson's personal leadership was more important than his tactical genius at Trafalgar.' How far do you agree? Use specific evidence.
💎 PlatinumNelson had affairs, ignored orders, and craved glory. Does this make him less of a hero? Research two historians' views and write a balanced 16-mark essay: 'How far does Nelson deserve his reputation as Britain's greatest naval hero?'
⚡ Quick Quiz
What famous signal did Nelson send before the Battle of Trafalgar?
🏛️ Historian's Verdict “Nelson's genius was not just tactical — it was psychological. He made his captains understand his mind so thoroughly that they could act without orders.” — Andrew Lambert, 2004
💡 Did You Know?
The Battle of Trafalgar lasted only 5 hours.
🧠

🌍 Europe in 1914 — A Continent on a Knife Edge

In the summer of 1914, Europe was the most powerful, prosperous, and dangerous place on earth. Decades of rivalry, imperial competition, and military build-up had created a situation where a single spark could ignite a continent-wide war. That spark came on 28 June 1914 — in a street in Sarajevo, Bosnia.

💥 M.A.I.N. — The Four Long-Term Causes

Historians use the acronym M.A.I.N. to describe the four deep, long-term forces that pushed Europe towards war. These causes built up over decades before the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand provided the final trigger.

⚔️
Militarism
European powers — especially Germany and Britain — competed in a massive arms race. Germany built a huge navy; Britain responded by building Dreadnoughts. By 1914, Europe was armed to the teeth and generals were eager to use their new weapons.
🤝
Alliances
Europe had split into two armed camps: the Triple Alliance (Germany, Austria-Hungary, Italy) and the Triple Entente (France, Russia, Britain). These alliances meant a local dispute could drag the whole continent into war.
🌍
Imperialism
European powers competed fiercely for colonies in Africa and Asia. Morocco crises (1905, 1911) brought France and Germany close to war. Imperial rivalry created deep resentments and distrust.
🏴
Nationalism
Intense pride in one's nation drove dangerous rivalries. In the Balkans (the 'powder keg of Europe'), Slavic peoples wanted independence from Austria-Hungary. Serbian nationalism directly triggered the 1914 crisis.

🔫 The Spark — Sarajevo, 28 June 1914

Archduke Franz Ferdinand, heir to the Austro-Hungarian throne, was assassinated in Sarajevo by Gavrilo Princip — a Bosnian Serb nationalist. It was supposed to be a routine royal visit. It became the trigger for the deadliest conflict in human history.

📅 The July Crisis — How a Murder Became a World War
28 JunArchduke Franz Ferdinand assassinated in Sarajevo by Gavrilo Princip
23 JulAustria-Hungary issues ultimatum to Serbia — 48 hours to accept humiliating terms
28 JulAustria-Hungary declares war on Serbia
1 AugGermany declares war on Russia (alliance system kicks in)
3 AugGermany declares war on France; invades Belgium
4 AugBritain declares war on Germany (protecting Belgian neutrality)
📜 Gavrilo Princip, interview after his arrest, 1914
"I am not a criminal, for I have done away with an evil man. I meant to do a good deed."

Apply WACA: Princip was under arrest when he said this. How does this affect the source's reliability? What was his aim in saying it?

🪖 Life in the Trenches — The Western Front

By Christmas 1914, the German advance had been halted and both sides had dug trenches stretching from the English Channel to Switzerland — over 700km. For four years, millions of men lived and died in these narrow ditches of mud.

😰 The Realities
  • 💧 Mud, water, and rats were constant companions
  • 🦟 Trench foot (flesh rotting in wet boots) affected thousands
  • 💣 Artillery barrages could last days — constant noise and terror
  • ⏱️ Most men spent 90% of their time waiting, not fighting
  • 💀 A single day at the Somme (1 July 1916): 57,470 British casualties
🔬 New Weapons
  • ☠️ Poison gas: first used by Germany at Ypres, April 1915
  • 🚜 Tanks: introduced by Britain at the Somme, 1916
  • ✈️ Aeroplanes: reconnaissance first, then dogfights and bombing
  • 💣 Heavy artillery: the defining weapon of WW1
  • 🌊 Submarines: Germany's U-boats threatened to starve Britain
📜 Wilfred Owen, 'Dulce et Decorum Est', 1917
"Gas! Gas! Quick, boys! — An ecstasy of fumbling / Fitting the clumsy helmets just in time; / But someone still was yelling out and stumbling / And flound'ring like a man in fire or lime."

Wilfred Owen was a soldier and poet who served on the Western Front. Apply WACA: how does knowing he experienced this directly affect the source's value?

🕊️ The End of the War and Its Legacy

By November 1918, four years of industrial-scale slaughter had exhausted all sides. Germany's allies had collapsed, her army was retreating, and revolution threatened at home. The Armistice was signed at 11am on 11 November 1918. But the peace — embodied in the Treaty of Versailles — would create the conditions for an even deadlier conflict just 21 years later.

📊 The Human Cost
  • 💀 17 million dead (military and civilian)
  • 🤕 21 million wounded
  • 🌍 Four empires destroyed: Ottoman, Austro-Hungarian, Russian, German
  • 🗺️ The map of Europe completely redrawn
📜 Treaty of Versailles — The Seeds of WW2
  • 😤 Germany forced to accept sole blame (War Guilt Clause)
  • 💰 £6.6 billion in reparations — crippling the German economy
  • ✂️ Germany lost 13% of its territory and all its colonies
  • ⚔️ German army reduced to 100,000 men
  • 😡 Most Germans saw it as a humiliation — Hitler exploited this
🎯 Activity 1: M.A.I.N. Analysis
🎯 Activity 2: The July Crisis
  • Create a step-by-step flowchart showing how the assassination of Franz Ferdinand led to a world war within 6 weeks
  • At each step, explain: WHY this happened as a consequence of the previous step
  • Could the war have been stopped at any point in the chain? Where? By whom?
  • Apply WACA to the Princip source above. Is this a reliable account of his motives?
🎯 Activity 3: Life in the Trenches
  • You are a British soldier on the Western Front in 1916. Write a letter home describing your daily life — include specific details about conditions, weapons, and your feelings
  • Apply WACA to the Wilfred Owen source. How does his identity as a soldier-poet affect how we use this source?
  • Is poetry a useful historical source? What can it tell us that official reports cannot? What are its limitations?
🎯 Activity 4: The Treaty of Versailles
  • Study the five terms of the Treaty of Versailles listed above. For each term: explain why the Allies imposed it AND why Germans resented it
  • Was the Treaty of Versailles too harsh, too lenient, or about right? Write a balanced argument
  • 💎 Platinum: The historian A.J.P. Taylor argued that Hitler was largely a response to Versailles. How far do you agree? What other factors led to WW2?
⚡ Quick Quiz
What does the 'N' in M.A.I.N. stand for?
🏛️ Historian's Verdict “The war was the greatest catastrophe the world has ever seen — destroying the Victorian certainty that progress was inevitable.” — John Keegan, 1998
💡 Did You Know?
On 1 July 1916 Britain suffered 57,470 casualties — day one of the Somme.
🧠

💬
Blog Spot
Make a claim · Support it with evidence · Respond like a historian
Was World War One caused more by long-term tensions than by the Sarajevo assassination?
🎥 Dan Snow · The History Guy · Crash Course
🎥 Dan Snow: World War One — What Started It?🎥 History Guy: Life in the Trenches WW1🎥 Crash Course: WW1 — Crash Course World History
📂 Lesson Files
  • 📊 WW1 Causes PPT
  • 📊 WW1 Trenches PPT
  • 📁 WW1 PDF
  • 📄 WW1 Research Document
  • 📄 WW1 Sources & Analysis
  • 📄 WW1 Essay Skills Guide
🔗 Research Links
  • 🔗 Britannica: World War I
  • 🔗 Britannica: Treaty of Versailles
📘 T&L: Critical Thinking & Collaboration⭐ HPL: Analysing · Linking · Empathy🌍 SDG 12–15: Global Stewardship☮️ SDG 16: Peace & Justice🤖 AI: G8 Solution Builder

🕊️ Why Was the League Created?

The First World War killed 17 million people. When it ended in 1918, world leaders were determined to prevent such a catastrophe from ever happening again. US President Woodrow Wilson proposed a revolutionary idea: an international organisation where countries could settle their disputes peacefully. The League of Nations was born — the world's first attempt at collective security.

🎯 The League's AIMS
  • 🕊️ Prevent war through collective security
  • 🤝 Encourage disarmament among all nations
  • 🌍 Improve living and working conditions globally
  • 📜 Uphold the terms of the Treaty of Versailles
⚠️ Built-In Weaknesses
  • 🇺🇸 USA never joined — Senate refused to ratify
  • ⚔️ No army of its own — could only use economic sanctions
  • 🗳️ Decisions had to be unanimous — one veto blocked everything
  • 🚫 Germany excluded until 1926, USSR excluded entirely

🏛️ How Was the League Organised?

🏛️
The Assembly
All member states met here once a year. One nation, one vote. Decisions had to be unanimous — a single veto could block any action.
📋
The Council
Met more regularly. Britain, France, Italy, Japan were permanent members. 4-11 non-permanent members elected by Assembly.
⚖️
Court of Justice
Based in The Hague, Netherlands. 15 judges settled legal disputes between nations. Had no power to enforce its decisions.
🌍
Commissions
Specialised agencies dealt with global problems: Slavery, Health, Refugees, Labour Conditions. Some achieved remarkable results.

✅ Successes of the 1920s

In the 1920s, when most countries still wanted peace, the League had real successes. But these tended to be smaller disputes between weaker nations — not the great power conflicts it had been designed to prevent.

🏆 Key Successes
1921
Aaland Islands dispute (Finland vs Sweden): League decided in Finland's favour. Both sides accepted. SUCCESS.
1921
Upper Silesia (Germany vs Poland): League divided the territory after a referendum. Both accepted. SUCCESS.
1922
Greek-Turkish refugee crisis: League helped resettle 400,000 refugees. SUCCESS.
1925
Greece vs Bulgaria: Greece invaded; League ordered withdrawal and fined Greece. Both accepted. SUCCESS.
1920s
Commissions: Freed 200,000 slaves in Sierra Leone. Returned 400,000 WW1 prisoners. Fought malaria globally.

❌ Failures of the 1930s — Collapse of Collective Security

The Wall Street Crash of 1929 shattered the fragile peace. Economic desperation brought aggressive nationalist governments to power. The League was tested by major powers — and failed completely.

💔 Key Failures
1923
Corfu Crisis: Italy bombarded Greek island of Corfu. League condemned it but backed down when Italy threatened to leave. FAILURE.
1931
Manchuria: Japan invaded Chinese Manchuria. League took 18 months to investigate. Japan simply left the League. FAILURE.
1935-36
Abyssinia: Italy invaded Abyssinia (Ethiopia). League imposed sanctions — but excluded oil, the one thing that would have worked. Britain and France secretly offered Italy territory. CATASTROPHIC FAILURE.
1936
Remilitarisation: Germany remilitarised the Rhineland. League did nothing. Emboldened Hitler for further aggression. FAILURE.
📜 Haile Selassie, Emperor of Abyssinia, speech to the League, 1936
"It is us today. It will be you tomorrow. Apart from the Kingdom of God, there is not on this earth any nation that is higher than any other."

Apply WACA: Who was Selassie? Why was he addressing the League? What was his aim? How does knowing Ethiopia had just been conquered affect this source?

🔍 Why Did the League Fail?

🇺🇸
USA Absent
The world's most powerful nation never joined. Without US economic and military backing, the League lacked credibility and power.
⚔️
No Army
The League could only impose economic sanctions — and even these required unanimous agreement. It had no teeth.
🗳️
Unanimous Decisions
Any single member could veto action. This made decisive responses to aggression almost impossible.
💰
Economic Depression
After 1929, member states prioritised their own economic recovery over collective action against aggressors.
🏛️
Self-Interest
Britain and France — the League's leaders — often prioritised their own interests over enforcing League rules (see: Hoare-Laval Pact).
🎯 Activity 1: Successes and Failures
🎯 Activity 2: Why Did the League Fail?
  • Look at the five reasons for the League's failure in the cards above. Rank them from most to least important and justify each ranking
  • Which failure (Manchuria, Abyssinia, Corfu, Rhineland) was most damaging to the League's credibility? Explain your answer
  • Was failure inevitable from the start — or did the League genuinely have a chance to succeed? Argue both sides
🎯 Activity 3: Independent Study — Causes of WW2

The failure of the League was one reason for WW2. But there were others. Research ONE of the following and present your findings:

  • (a) The Treaty of Versailles — how did its terms help cause WW2?
  • (b) The Great Depression — how did economic crisis lead to the rise of Hitler?
  • (c) Appeasement — was Chamberlain's policy sensible or cowardly?
  • (d) Hitler's foreign policy 1936-39 — a calculated plan or seizing opportunities?

💎 Platinum: 'The failure of the League of Nations was the most significant cause of World War Two.' How far do you agree? Write a 16-mark essay considering at least three different causes.

⚡ Quick Quiz
Why did the USA never join the League of Nations?
🏛️ Historian's Verdict “The League was a noble experiment that failed — not because the idea was wrong, but because the nations who needed it most refused to make it work.” — Sally Marks
💡 Did You Know?
The League had 63 members — but never included the USA.
🧠

🎯 Year 10 & 11 — iGCSE History

Cold War · Stalin's Russia · Vietnam War · Changes in Medicine

⚡ iGCSE Exam Practice Questions

Cycle through 6, 8 and 16-mark questions. Write or speak your answer, then click Get Feedback for personalised AI guidance.

📘 Paper 1 — Cold War & Stalin
📝 Mark scheme tip
📗 Paper 2 — Vietnam & Medicine
📝 Mark scheme tip
✍️
iGCSE Answer Marker
Mark your practice essays against official Edexcel level descriptors. Covers every topic on the 4HI1 specification. Model answer unlocked only after resubmission.

📐 Edexcel iGCSE — Question Structure Guide

📅 Paper 1 (Cold War & Stalin): Friday 16 May  ·  Paper 2 (Vietnam / Medicine): Thursday 5 June

6-Mark: Interpret

What impression does the author give? Analyse HOW the author creates the impression through language choices and selection of evidence.

8-Mark: Source Utility

WACA provenance + specific quotes + own knowledge. ALL THREE required. Quote exact words — never paraphrase.

16-Mark: Essay

Intro (thesis) → Para 1 (bullet 1) → Para 2 (bullet 2) → Para 3 (own info) → Conclusion. THREE ASPECTS required or marks are capped.

🌍 Why Did the Cold War Begin?

The Cold War was not a single moment but a slow collision of two incompatible world visions. By 1945, the USA and USSR had defeated Nazi Germany together — but their ideological differences made genuine peace impossible. What followed was nearly five decades of tension, proxy wars, and near-apocalyptic confrontation.

🗽 USA — Capitalism
  • 🏭 Free market economy — private ownership
  • 🗳️ Multi-party democracy and individual freedoms
  • 💰 Wealth distribution through market competition
  • 🌐 Promoted democracy and open trade globally
  • ☢️ Held atomic monopoly 1945–49
☭ USSR — Communism
  • 🏗️ State-controlled economy — collective ownership
  • 🎖️ One-party state led by the Communist Party
  • ⚖️ Wealth distributed by the state
  • 🌍 Promoted communist revolution worldwide
  • 🛡️ Surrounded by hostile capitalist powers

📅 The Grand Alliance Breaks Down (1943–46)

Wartime co-operation masked deep mistrust. The three key conferences — Tehran, Yalta, and Potsdam — revealed growing tensions that would define the post-war world.

1943Tehran Conference: Stalin, Roosevelt, Churchill agree on D-Day invasion. Stalin demands a buffer zone of friendly states on Soviet borders.
Feb 1945Yalta Conference: Germany to be divided into occupation zones. Free elections promised for Eastern Europe. USSR agrees to join Pacific War.
Jul 1945Potsdam Conference: Truman (new US president) informed Stalin of the atomic bomb. Disagreements over Germany's future and reparations grow sharply.
Mar 1946Churchill's 'Iron Curtain' speech: 'From Stettin in the Baltic to Trieste in the Adriatic, an iron curtain has descended across the Continent.'
📜 The Long Telegram — George Kennan, US diplomat in Moscow, February 1946
"Soviet power is impervious to the logic of reason... it is highly sensitive to the logic of force. For this reason it can easily withdraw — and usually does — when strong resistance is encountered at any point."

Kennan's telegram shaped US foreign policy. Apply WACA: who wrote it? For whom? What did Truman do with it?

🎯 Activity 1: Yalta vs Potsdam — What Changed?

🔒 Containment and the Berlin Crisis (1947–49)

The Truman Doctrine (1947) committed the USA to containing communism wherever it threatened to spread. The Marshall Plan provided $13 billion to rebuild Western Europe — and prevent communist parties from exploiting poverty.

📋
Truman Doctrine (1947)
USA would support 'free peoples who are resisting subjugation.' Directed immediately at Greece and Turkey — but applied globally thereafter.
💰
Marshall Plan (1947)
$13 billion in economic aid to rebuild Western Europe. Stalin saw it as 'dollar imperialism' — forbade Eastern European states from accepting it.
🚫
Cominform (1947)
Soviet response: coordinating body for communist parties across Eastern Europe. Ensured loyalty to Moscow over national interests.
⚡
Berlin Blockade (1948–49)
Stalin blockaded West Berlin — cutting off all road, rail and canal links. Western response: the Berlin Airlift delivered 170,000 tonnes of supplies in January 1949 alone.
📜 Telegram from Stalin, June 1948 (reconstructed from Soviet archives)
"Berlin is located in the Soviet zone and is part of that zone. We will not permit the Western powers to have communications through our zone that are not under our control."

Apply WACA: Stalin's official justification for the blockade. Is this the real reason — or a legal pretext?

🎯 Activity 2: Berlin Blockade Analysis
  • Why did Stalin choose to blockade Berlin in 1948? Identify at least THREE possible motives.
  • Why did Truman choose the airlift rather than a direct military challenge? What were the risks of each option?
  • 🥇 'The Berlin Blockade (1948–49) was a turning point in the Cold War.' How far do you agree? Consider what changed and what stayed the same. 💎 Write a full 8-mark answer with a clear judgement.

💥 Flashpoints (1950–62): Korea to Cuba

1950–53Korean War: USA/UN intervenes to defend South Korea from communist North. China enters in November 1950. Ends in stalemate at the 38th Parallel.
1953Death of Stalin. Khrushchev emerges as Soviet leader — promises 'peaceful coexistence' but still competes aggressively.
1956Hungarian Uprising crushed by Soviet tanks. Imre Nagy executed. West protests but does nothing — containment has limits.
1957Sputnik: USSR launches first satellite. USA shocked — the Soviets can now hit American cities with nuclear missiles.
1961Berlin Wall constructed overnight 13 August. Stops refugee crisis — 3.5 million had fled West since 1949.
Oct 1962Cuban Missile Crisis: 13 days that brought the world closest to nuclear war. Khrushchev places missiles in Cuba; Kennedy demands removal.
📜 Kennedy's broadcast to the American people, 22 October 1962
"It shall be the policy of this nation to regard any nuclear missile launched from Cuba against any nation in the Western Hemisphere as an attack on the United States, requiring a full retaliatory response upon the Soviet Union."

Apply WACA: this was broadcast to the American public AND intended for Khrushchev. How does knowing both audiences change our interpretation?

🎯 Activity 3: Cuban Missile Crisis — Decision-Making Under Pressure
  • Construct a timeline of the 13 days of the Cuban Missile Crisis (16–28 October 1962). Include: the discovery of missiles, Kennedy's options (invasion, air strike, blockade, negotiation), Khrushchev's responses.
  • Why did Kennedy choose a naval blockade rather than an air strike? What were the risks of each option?
  • 🥇 'Kennedy handled the Cuban Missile Crisis better than Khrushchev.' How far do you agree? 💎 Write an 8-mark answer considering the actions and motives of both leaders.

🕊️ Détente and the End of Superpower Rivalry (1963–72)

After Cuba, both superpowers recognised the danger of direct confrontation. A period of careful de-escalation began — though the underlying competition never stopped.

📞
Hot Line (1963)
Direct telephone link between Moscow and Washington. Never used publicly — but its existence reduced the risk of accidental nuclear war.
☢️
Test Ban Treaty (1963)
Banned nuclear testing in the atmosphere, underwater, and in space. A first step — but underground testing continued.
🤝
Non-Proliferation Treaty (1968)
USA and USSR agreed not to spread nuclear weapons to other countries. Neither fully kept the spirit of the agreement.
📋
SALT I (1972)
Strategic Arms Limitation Talks — Nixon and Brezhnev agree to cap numbers of certain nuclear weapons. First formal arms control agreement.
🎯 Activity 4: Was Détente Real Peace?
⚡ Exam Practice — Cold War & Superpower Relations
The Cold War is Paper 1, Section D4. Expect: 4-mark, 6-mark, 8-mark and 16-mark questions.
🥈 SilverExplain why the Berlin Blockade (1948–49) increased Cold War tensions. Aim for 2–3 developed reasons with named evidence.
🥇 Gold'The Cuban Missile Crisis (1962) was the most dangerous moment of the Cold War.' How far do you agree? Consider: Berlin Blockade, Korean War, Hungarian Uprising, Cuba. Reach a justified judgement.
💎 Platinum'Soviet aggression was the main cause of the Cold War.' How far do you agree? Write a 16-mark essay with THREE aspects: ideology, Soviet expansion, US response. Use specific evidence throughout and reach a justified conclusion.
🏛️ Historian's Verdict “We had eyeball to eyeball with the Russians, and I think the other fellow just blinked first.” — Dean Rusk, October 1962
🧠

💬

Make a claim · Support it with evidence · Respond like a historian
Was the USA or the USSR more responsible for the tensions that led to the Cold War?
🎥 Dan Snow · The History Guy · Crash Course
🎥 Dan Snow: The Cold War Explained🎥 The History Guy: Cuban Missile Crisis🎥 Crash Course: The Cold War
📂 Lesson Files
  • 📊 Cold War Overview PPT
  • 📊 Cold War Week 1 PPT
  • 📊 Cold War Week 2 PPT
  • 📊 Cold War Week 3 PPT
  • 📊 Cold War Week 4-5 PPT
  • 📁 Superpower Relations PDF
🔗 Research Links
  • 🔗 Britannica: The Cold War
  • 🔗 Britannica: Cuban Missile Crisis
  • 🔗 Imperial War Museum: Cold War
  • 🔗 BBC Bitesize: Cold War
💡 Did You Know?
During the Cuban Missile Crisis Soviet submarines were ready to launch nuclear torpedoes.

⚔️ How Stalin Rose to Power (1924–29)

When Lenin died in January 1924, the USSR faced a succession crisis. Lenin himself had warned the Party about Stalin in his Testament — calling him "too rude" and recommending his removal. The Politburo suppressed it. Within five years, Stalin had outmanoeuvred every rival and stood unchallenged as the most powerful man in the Soviet Union.

📜 Lenin's Testament — postscript, 4 January 1923
"Stalin is too rude, and this fault... becomes unbearable in the office of General Secretary. Therefore I propose to the comrades to find a way of removing Stalin from that post."

Apply WACA: written by Lenin shortly before his death. Why did the Politburo suppress it? What does this tell us about how Soviet politics operated?

Why did Stalin win? He used his position as General Secretary to control Party appointments — placing loyal supporters in key posts across the country. He adopted "Socialism in One Country" — a popular, practical alternative to Trotsky's "Permanent Revolution." He played rivals off against each other: allying with the Right to destroy the Left, then turning on the Right. His rivals, convinced he was a mediocrity, fatally underestimated him.

🗡️
The Left Defeated
1924–27: Trotsky, Zinoviev and Kamenev form the United Opposition. Stalin outmanoeuvres them using Party machinery. Trotsky expelled 1927, exiled to Kazakhstan 1928, eventually to Mexico where he was assassinated in 1940.
⚙️
The Right Defeated
1928–29: Stalin turns on his former allies Bukharin, Rykov and Tomsky over the NEP. Accuses them of 'right deviation.' By his 50th birthday, December 1929, Stalin rules alone.
🎖️
General Secretary
Since 1922, Stalin controlled party appointments nationwide. This gave him thousands of loyal supporters — the real foundation of his power.
🌍
Socialism in One Country
Focus on building socialism in the USSR first, before exporting revolution. Contrasted with Trotsky's Permanent Revolution — seen as more realistic and achievable.
🎯 Activity 1: Why Did Stalin Win the Power Struggle?
  • Rank the following factors in Stalin's rise to power from most to least important: (a) his position as General Secretary; (b) his political cunning; (c) the mistakes of his rivals; (d) his policies (Socialism in One Country). Justify each ranking.
  • Apply WACA to Lenin's Testament. Why was it suppressed — and what does this tell us about power in the early Soviet Union?
  • 🥇 'Stalin's position as General Secretary was the most important reason he won the power struggle.' How far do you agree? 💎 Write an 8-mark answer using specific evidence.

🏭 The Five-Year Plans and Collectivisation

By 1928, Stalin had decided the USSR must industrialise at breakneck speed — or face destruction at the hands of capitalist powers. "We are fifty or a hundred years behind the advanced countries," he told factory workers in 1931. "We must make good this distance in ten years. Either we do it, or we shall be crushed."

🏭 Industrial Achievements
  • ⚡ Electricity production increased NINE times (1928–40)
  • 🔩 Steel production trebled (4M to 18M tonnes)
  • 🏙️ New cities: Magnitogorsk built from nothing in 5 years
  • 🎖️ USSR became the world's 3rd largest industrial power by 1940
  • ⚔️ Military-industrial base that would defeat Hitler
💀 Human Cost
  • 🌾 Collectivisation: 5–7 million died in Ukrainian famine (Holodomor, 1932–33)
  • 🐴 Peasants slaughtered livestock rather than hand it to collectives
  • ⛏️ Gulags: 18 million sent to labour camps 1930–53; 1.5 million died
  • 👷 Stakhanovite workers praised; those who failed quotas punished
  • 🔫 Kulaks (wealthier peasants) "liquidated as a class"
🎯 Activity 2: Was Industrialisation Worth the Human Cost?
  • Using the data above, construct a balance sheet: list the ACHIEVEMENTS of the Five-Year Plans against the HUMAN COSTS. Which side is heavier — and why?
  • Stalin said the USSR must industrialise or be crushed. Was he right? Consider: what happened when Germany invaded in 1941.
  • 🥇 'The Five-Year Plans modernised the USSR but at an unacceptable human cost.' How far do you agree? 💎 Write an 8-mark answer that considers both the achievements AND the suffering.

☠️ The Terror and the Great Purge (1936–38)

Between 1936 and 1938, Stalin launched the most systematic campaign of political murder in Soviet history. Show trials — carefully staged public confessions — convicted the original Bolshevik leaders of fantastic crimes. The NKVD (secret police) arrested millions. Fear became the operating principle of Soviet society.

Dec 1934Assassination of Kirov — pretext for the Purge. Historians debate whether Stalin ordered it.
1936First show trial: Zinoviev and Kamenev 'confess' to plotting assassination and are shot.
1937Marshal Tukhachevsky and 7 top generals executed — the Red Army loses 35,000 officers.
1937–38The Great Terror: 1.5 million arrested; 750,000 executed; 18 million passed through Gulag system.
1938Third show trial: Bukharin and Rykov confess and are shot. Original Bolshevik leadership eliminated.
📜 Arthur Koestler, Darkness at Noon, 1940 (fictionalised account based on show trials)
"He had read and re-read the confessions of the others, who all followed the same pattern: the Party is always right; I have sinned against the Party; I must serve the Party's interests even in my death."

Koestler was himself a communist who broke with the party. Apply WACA: is a fictional account useful as historical evidence? What can it tell us that official records cannot?

🎯 Activity 3: Why Did Stalin Launch the Great Purge?
  • Historians disagree about Stalin's motives. Evaluate each explanation: (a) genuine fear of opposition; (b) paranoia and psychological instability; (c) desire to eliminate potential rivals; (d) scapegoating for economic failures; (e) consolidation of personal power.
  • Why did the accused confess at the show trials? (Consider: torture, threats to family, psychological manipulation, genuine belief in the Party.)
  • 🥇 'The Great Purge was primarily about eliminating political rivals.' How far do you agree? 💎 Write a 16-mark essay with THREE aspects and a justified conclusion.

🎨 Propaganda, Cult of Personality and Life under Stalin

Terror alone could not sustain Stalin's power. Alongside fear, the regime constructed one of the most sophisticated propaganda machines in history — rewriting textbooks, airbrushing photographs, and cultivating a near-religious cult of Stalin as the all-knowing "Father of Nations."

📚
Education
All textbooks rewritten to glorify Stalin and communism. History falsified: photographs altered to remove purged officials. Children encouraged to report anti-Soviet comments by parents.
🎨
Socialist Realism
All art, literature and music must depict the heroic Soviet worker building communism. Anything 'decadent' or critical was forbidden — artists who stepped out of line disappeared.
📰
Censorship
All media state-controlled. Foreign radio jammed. Citizens who told anti-Stalin jokes could be arrested. Private correspondence read by NKVD.
🏆
Stalin as Father of Nations
Portraits in every public building. Cities renamed: Stalingrad, Stalinabad. Stalin presented as omniscient guide who personally ensured the USSR's survival and progress.
🎯 Activity 4: How Did Stalin Maintain Power?
🏛️ Historian's Verdict “The key to understanding Stalin's power is the gap between the image and the reality. Millions lived inside that gap — and millions died there.” — Simon Sebag Montefiore, 2003
🧠

Was terror more important than propaganda in maintaining Stalin's power?

  • 📁 Stalin Ch.3 — Terror
  • 📁 Stalin Ch.4 — Society
  • 📁 Stalin Ch.5 — Legacy
🔗 Research Links
  • 🔗 Britannica: Joseph Stalin
  • 🔗 Britannica: The Gulag
  • 🔗 BBC Bitesize: GCSE Stalin
  • 🔗 Britannica: Collectivisation
💡 Did You Know?
Five-Year Plans increased Soviet steel from 4 to 18 million tonnes 1928-1940.

🌏 Why Did the USA Get Involved in Vietnam?

After the French defeat at Dien Bien Phu (1954), Vietnam was temporarily divided at the 17th parallel — communist North under Ho Chi Minh, pro-Western South under Diem. The USA feared that if South Vietnam fell to communism, the rest of South-East Asia would follow — the Domino Theory. What began as financial aid and military advisors would escalate into America's longest and most divisive war.

🀱
Domino Theory
Eisenhower (1954): if one country falls to communism, its neighbours will follow like dominoes. Vietnam was seen as the crucial first domino in South-East Asia.
💰
Financial Aid
USA began funding the French war effort from 1950. By 1954, paying 80% of French war costs — yet France still lost at Dien Bien Phu.
🪖
Military Advisors
Kennedy sent 16,000 'military advisors' by 1963. Officially not combat troops — actually training the ARVN (Army of the Republic of Vietnam).
💥
Gulf of Tonkin (1964)
Two reported attacks on US destroyers (one probably never happened). Congress passed the Gulf of Tonkin Resolution — giving Johnson blank-cheque authority to escalate without declaring war.
📜 President Eisenhower — press conference, April 1954
"You have a row of dominoes set up, you knock over the first one, and what will happen to the last one is a certainty — it will go over very quickly."

Apply WACA: this is the Domino Theory explained by the president himself. Was it an accurate analysis — or a political justification for intervention?

🎯 Activity 1: Why Did the USA Escalate Involvement?
  • Create a timeline of US escalation: 1950 (financial aid) → 1955 (military advisors) → 1964 (Gulf of Tonkin) → 1965 (first combat troops) → 1968 (peak 540,000 troops). For each step, explain WHY the USA went further.
  • Was the Gulf of Tonkin incident a genuine attack or a pretext? What evidence exists on both sides?
  • 🥇 'The Domino Theory was the main reason the USA became involved in Vietnam.' How far do you agree? 💎 Write a 6-mark answer using specific evidence.

⚔️ US Military Tactics — Search and Destroy

By 1965, 540,000 US troops were in Vietnam. General Westmoreland's strategy was "attrition" — killing enough North Vietnamese and Viet Cong to break their will to fight. It failed — because the enemy's will to fight proved inexhaustible.

🔥 US Tactics
  • 🚁 Search & Destroy: helicopter-borne raids on suspected Viet Cong villages
  • 🌿 Agent Orange: herbicide defoliation — 80 million litres dropped, destroying jungle cover and crops
  • 💣 Napalm bombing: incendiary gel — stuck to skin and burned at 1,000°C
  • 🏗️ Strategic Hamlets: moving peasants into fortified villages — failed, created resentment
  • 📊 Body Count: military success measured by enemy dead — encouraged false reporting
🌿 Viet Cong Tactics
  • 🕳️ Cu Chi Tunnels: 250km network — storage, hospitals, troop movement
  • 💣 Booby traps: punji sticks, tripwires — caused 11% of US casualties
  • 🌳 Guerrilla warfare: attack and melt into civilian population
  • 🏘️ Hearts & Minds: political work among peasants to win local support
  • 🛣️ Ho Chi Minh Trail: 1,000km supply route through Laos/Cambodia
📜 Philip Caputo, US Marine, A Rumour of War, 1977
"We were not in Vietnam to win. We were there to demonstrate that America was willing to fight, even if it couldn't win. And we were killing people — lots of people — but nothing was changing."

Apply WACA: Caputo was a participant — eyewitness value high, but written 12 years after events. How does PTSD and retrospective analysis affect reliability?

🎯 Activity 2: Why Did US Tactics Fail?
  • For each US tactic (Search & Destroy, Agent Orange, Strategic Hamlets, napalm), explain: what it was designed to achieve AND why it failed.
  • How did the Viet Cong's guerrilla tactics neutralise America's technological advantages?
  • 🥇 'US military tactics in Vietnam were the main reason the USA failed to win the war.' How far do you agree? 💎 Write an 8-mark answer with a balanced evaluation.

📺 The Tet Offensive and the Credibility Gap (1968)

On 31 January 1968 — the Vietnamese New Year (Tet) — 85,000 Viet Cong and North Vietnamese troops simultaneously attacked 100+ cities across South Vietnam. US and South Vietnamese forces regained all the ground within weeks. But the political damage was permanent.

📜 Walter Cronkite — CBS Evening News broadcast, February 1968
"It seems now more certain than ever that the bloody experience of Vietnam is to end in a stalemate... To say that we are closer to victory today is to believe, in the face of the evidence, the optimists who have been wrong in the past."

Cronkite was 'the most trusted man in America.' When Johnson heard this broadcast, he reportedly said: 'If I've lost Cronkite, I've lost Middle America.' Apply WACA to this source.

🎯 Activity 3: How Did Tet Change Everything?
  • Before Tet, Johnson had been saying the USA was 'winning' — the 'light at the end of the tunnel.' How did Tet expose this as the Credibility Gap?
  • Why was Tet a military failure for the Viet Cong but a political victory? What does this tell us about the nature of the Vietnam War?
  • 🥇 'The Tet Offensive (1968) was the turning point of the Vietnam War.' How far do you agree? 💎 Write an 8-mark answer.

✈️ Withdrawal and the Legacy of Vietnam

Nixon's strategy of "Vietnamisation" — gradually handing the fighting back to South Vietnamese forces while withdrawing US troops — preserved American pride but could not prevent the inevitable. Saigon fell to communist forces on 30 April 1975.

🔄
Vietnamisation
Nixon withdraws US troops gradually — 540,000 in 1968 to 50,000 by 1972. But the ARVN cannot hold without US support.
💣
Cambodia Bombing
Nixon secretly bombs Cambodia to destroy Ho Chi Minh Trail. Leaked to press — triggers massive antiwar protests including Kent State (4 students killed by National Guard).
📋
Paris Peace Accords (1973)
Ceasefire agreement — US troops withdraw. South Vietnam government told to survive alone. It cannot.
🏃
Fall of Saigon (1975)
North Vietnamese forces enter Saigon. US helicopters evacuate the last Americans from the embassy rooftop. The defining image of American defeat.
🎯 Activity 4: Why Did the USA Fail in Vietnam?
🏛️ Historian's Verdict “We did not have the best equipment. But we had something the Americans could not buy — we were fighting for our home.” — North Vietnamese veteran, 1990
🧠
Socrates, your historical AI tutor
Guides you · Socratic questioning · Essay & source skills

💬
Blog Spot
Make a claim · Support it with evidence · Respond like a historian
Was the Tet Offensive the turning point of the Vietnam War — or just the moment the truth became visible?
🎥 Dan Snow · The History Guy · Crash Course
🎥 Dan Snow: Vietnam War Explained🎥 History Guy: Tet Offensive 1968🎥 Crash Course: The Vietnam War
📂 Lesson Files
  • 📊 Vietnam PPT 1
  • 📊 Vietnam PPT 2
  • 📊 Vietnam PPT 3
  • 📊 Vietnam PPT 4
  • 📊 Vietnam PPT 5
🔗 Research Links
  • 🔗 Britannica: Vietnam War
  • 🔗 Britannica: Tet Offensive
  • 🔗 BBC Bitesize: GCSE Vietnam
  • 🔗 Imperial War Museum: Vietnam
💡 Did You Know?
The Cu Chi tunnel network (250km) included hospitals, command centres and schools.

🏥 Medicine in the Mid-Nineteenth Century (c.1848)

In 1848, medicine was a dangerous profession — not because doctors were incompetent, but because they lacked the fundamental knowledge that would transform their field. Without germ theory, surgeons operated in dirty aprons. Without anaesthetics, speed was the surgeon's only mercy. Without antiseptics, patients who survived the knife often died of infection. The next century would change everything.

❌ What Doctors Didn't Know (1848)
  • 🦠 Cause of disease: still believed in miasma (bad air)
  • 😷 Infection: surgeons operated in street clothes with bare hands
  • 💊 Antibiotics: had not been invented
  • 🩸 Blood types: not discovered until 1901
  • 📊 Statistics: no systematic data collection on outcomes
✅ What WAS Improving (1848)
  • 💤 Anaesthetics: Simpson uses chloroform 1847 — pain-free operations begin
  • 💉 Vaccination: Jenner's smallpox vaccine (1796) now widely used
  • 🏥 Nightingale: nursing reforms in Scutari during Crimean War (1854–56)
  • 🚰 Chadwick: Sanitary Report 1842 — first evidence for public health reform
  • 📊 Snow: broad Street Pump 1854 — proves cholera is waterborne
🎯 Activity 1: How Much Had Medicine Changed by 1848?

🔬 Pasteur, Koch and the Germ Theory Revolution (1860–1905)

The single most important development in the history of medicine was the Germ Theory — the discovery that specific microorganisms cause specific diseases. Once this was established, everything else followed: specific vaccines, specific treatments, targeted surgery.

1861Pasteur's Germ Theory — published his findings that microorganisms cause fermentation and decay. Disproves spontaneous generation.
1867Lister introduces carbolic acid antiseptic — surgical death rates from infection drop from 46% to 15%.
1875Koch identifies specific bacteria causing anthrax and tuberculosis — proving specific germs cause specific diseases.
1882Koch's postulates — systematic method for proving a microorganism causes a disease.
1880s–90sEhrlich and the 'magic bullet' — searching for chemical compounds that kill specific bacteria without harming the patient.
1909Salvarsan 606 — first chemotherapy drug, effective against syphilis. Proof that magic bullets were possible.
📜 Joseph Lister on his use of antiseptic technique, 1867
"All that is requisite is to prevent the access of the minute particles in the atmosphere to the wound... by covering with a material capable of killing those organisms."

Apply WACA: Lister was defending a controversial new technique — many doctors resisted it. What does resistance to Lister tell us about how medical knowledge changes?

🎯 Activity 2: Pasteur, Koch, Lister — Who Made the Biggest Contribution?
  • For each figure — Pasteur, Koch, Lister, Ehrlich — explain: their specific contribution AND its significance for medicine.
  • Why did it take so long for Pasteur's Germ Theory to be accepted? What does this tell us about how scientific knowledge changes?
  • 🥇 'Koch's work in identifying specific bacteria was more important than Pasteur's Germ Theory.' How far do you agree? 💎 Write a 6-mark answer with specific evidence.

🏛️ Government Action and World Wars (1905–1948)

Medical knowledge alone cannot improve public health — governments must act on it. The early 20th century saw the British government take unprecedented responsibility for citizens' health, driven partly by reformers, partly by the shocking state of army recruits during the Boer War.

🏫
Liberal Reforms (1906–11)
Free school meals, medical inspections, old age pensions, National Insurance. First time British government accepted responsibility for citizens' health.
🩺
World War One (1914–18)
Blood transfusions developed, plastic surgery pioneered, X-rays used on the front line, understanding of shell shock begins.
💊
Penicillin (1928–45)
Fleming discovers penicillin by accident (1928). Florey and Chain develop it into a usable drug by 1940. Mass-produced for D-Day — saves hundreds of thousands of lives.
🏥
NHS (1948)
Attlee's Labour government creates the National Health Service — free healthcare for all at the point of need. Life expectancy rises from 47 (1900) to 68 (1950).
📜 Aneurin Bevan, Minister of Health, on the creation of the NHS, 1948
"The NHS will last as long as there are folk left with the faith to fight for it."

Apply WACA: Bevan was the politician who created the NHS. Is this a primary or secondary source? How does his role affect its reliability as evidence of why the NHS was needed?

🎯 Activity 3: How Did the Government Change Medicine?
  • Why did the Boer War shock the British government into introducing the Liberal Reforms? What did it reveal about the nation's health?
  • How did each World War contribute to medical progress? Consider both the injuries treated AND the technologies developed.
  • 🥇 'The creation of the National Health Service (1948) was the most important factor in improving British public health in the 20th century.' How far do you agree? 💎 Write a 16-mark essay considering: the NHS, penicillin, public health legislation, and individual medical discoveries.

🧬 Modern Medicine (c.1920–c.1948) — The Age of Breakthroughs

The mid-20th century saw medicine transformed by a combination of factors: government funding, technological innovation, the demands of war, and occasional brilliant luck. The story of penicillin perfectly illustrates how chance, individual genius, government support, and technology must ALL combine for a medical revolution.

📜 Fleming's discovery of penicillin — reconstructed account, 1928
"When I woke up just after dawn on 28 September 1928, I certainly didn't plan to revolutionise all medicine by discovering the world's first antibiotic... But I suppose that was exactly what I did."

Apply WACA: this quote is widely cited but possibly apocryphal. How does it matter whether Fleming said this — or whether it was constructed later?

🧫 The Penicillin Story
  • 🔬 1928: Fleming notices mould killing bacteria — publishes finding, but cannot stabilise the drug
  • ⏸️ 1928–39: Penicillin ignored for a decade — no technology or funding to develop it
  • 🧪 1940: Florey and Chain (Oxford) develop a usable form — test on a police constable (he dies when supplies run out)
  • 🇺🇸 1943: USA mass-produces penicillin — government funding and wartime urgency make it possible
  • ⚔️ 1944: Used in D-Day invasion — revolutionises wound treatment
🎯 Activity 4: Factors in Medical Progress — What Matters Most?
🏛️ Historian's Verdict “The NHS was not a gift from politicians. It was the realisation of a right — that illness should not mean poverty.” — Aneurin Bevan, 1948
🧠

💡 Did You Know?
Fleming discovered penicillin in 1928 — BEFORE WW2. Florey made it usable in 1940.

⚡ iGCSE Exam Practice Questions

Cycle through 6, 8 and 16-mark questions. Write or speak your answer, then click Get Feedback for personalised AI guidance.

📘 Paper 1 — Cold War & Stalin
📝 Mark scheme tip
📗 Paper 2 — Vietnam & Medicine
📝 Mark scheme tip

What is History?

📅 Key Dates
Key methodWACA: Who, Audience, Context, Aim
Key skillChronology: BC counts BACKWARDS
Key conceptPRIMARY = created at the time. SECONDARY = created later
📖 Key Terms
Primary SourceSecondary SourceWACAChronologyCausationConsequenceChangeContinuity
💡 Exam Tips
💡 Apply WACA to EVERY source before judging its usefulness.
💡 BC counts BACKWARDS — 100 BC is BEFORE 50 BC.
💡 IMMEDIATE cause = the spark. UNDERLYING causes = deeper long-term reasons.
📝 Practice Exam Questions
Describe (4 marks)
Describe WACA as a method for analysing historical sources. Include: what each letter stands for, one example for each letter, and why applying WACA matters for historians.
Explain (6 marks)
Explain why historians need BOTH primary AND secondary sources when studying the past. Use two specific examples from the sources you have studied this year.
Evaluate (8 marks)
'A primary source is always more reliable than a secondary source.' How far do you agree? Consider: what makes a source reliable, how WACA reveals limitations, and give specific examples.
🧠

Make a claim · Support it with evidence · Respond like a historian
Can a source ever be both biased and genuinely useful as historical evidence?

Norman Conquest 1066

📅 Key Dates
14 Oct 1066Battle of Hastings — Harold killed, William wins
25 Sep 1066Battle of Stamford Bridge — Harold defeats Hardrada
1086Domesday Book — comprehensive land survey
📖 Key Terms
ProvenanceHousecarlsFyrdBayeux TapestryDomesday BookMotteBaileyFeudal SystemWACA
💡 Exam Tips
💡 WACA the Bayeux Tapestry — commissioned by Bishop Odo (William's half-brother). Does this matter?
💡 The Domesday Book shows the Conquest was an ADMINISTRATIVE revolution, not just military.
💡 Harold's three problems: forced march, tired army, died early.
📝 Practice Exam Questions
Describe (4 marks)
Describe the events of the Battle of Hastings on 14 October 1066. Include: the positions of both armies, the key turning point, and what happened to Harold Godwinson.
Explain (6 marks)
Explain why William won the Battle of Hastings in 1066. You should consider at least TWO reasons and use specific evidence.
Evaluate (8 marks)
'Harold Godwinson's own mistakes were the most important reason why the Norman Conquest was successful.' How far do you agree? Consider: Harold's mistakes, William's strengths, and the role of luck.

Were Harold Godwinson's own mistakes the MAIN reason William won at Hastings?

World War One 1914–18

📅 Key Dates
28 Jun 1914Franz Ferdinand assassinated at Sarajevo
1 Jul 1916Battle of the Somme — 57,470 British casualties on day one
11 Nov 1918Armistice
Jun 1919Treaty of Versailles — £6.6bn reparations
📖 Key Terms
M.A.I.N.MilitarismAlliancesImperialismNationalismSchlieffen PlanStalemateArmisticeReparations
💡 Exam Tips
💡 M.A.I.N. = Militarism, Alliances, Imperialism, Nationalism — UNDERLYING causes, not the spark.
💡 WACA both trench photographs: official IWM photographs — who took them? For whom? What purpose?
💡 Versailles imposed £6.6bn reparations — many historians link this to the rise of Hitler.
📝 Practice Exam Questions
Describe (4 marks)
Describe the conditions experienced by British soldiers in the trenches during World War One.
Explain (6 marks)
Explain the M.A.I.N. causes of World War One. For EACH cause: define it, give ONE specific dated example, and explain HOW it contributed to the outbreak of war.
Evaluate (8 marks)
'The assassination of Franz Ferdinand at Sarajevo was the most important cause of World War One.' How far do you agree?
🧠

Make a claim · Support it with evidence · Respond like a historian
Was World War One caused more by long-term tensions than by the Sarajevo assassination?

The League of Nations

📅 Key Dates
Jan 1920League of Nations formally founded
1931Japan invades Manchuria — League takes no effective action
Oct 1935Italy invades Abyssinia — sanctions exclude oil
Jun 1936Haile Selassie addresses the League
📖 Key Terms
Collective SecurityCovenantSanctionsUnanimityAbyssiniaManchuriaAppeasement
💡 Exam Tips
💡 USA never joined — the Senate refused to ratify the Treaty of Versailles by 49 votes to 35.
💡 League had NO ARMY — could only impose economic sanctions which aggressors could ignore.
💡 WACA the Haile Selassie photograph: who took it? For whom? What was his aim?
📝 Practice Exam Questions
Describe (4 marks)
Describe the failure of the League of Nations in response to the Italian invasion of Abyssinia 1935–36.
Explain (6 marks)
Explain TWO structural weaknesses of the League of Nations. For each: what it was AND what specific consequence it had.
Evaluate (8 marks)
'The USA's decision not to join the League of Nations was the most important reason for its failure.' How far do you agree?
🧠

Make a claim · Support it with evidence · Respond like a historian
Was the League of Nations structurally doomed to fail — or could better leadership have saved it?

Cold War 1943–72

📅 Key Dates
Mar 1946Churchill's Iron Curtain speech
Mar 1947Truman Doctrine
Jun 1948Berlin Blockade begins
Oct 1962Cuban Missile Crisis — 13 days
1972SALT I Treaty
📖 Key Terms
Iron CurtainContainmentTruman DoctrineMarshall PlanBerlin BlockadeNATOWarsaw PactMADDétenteSALT ILOOK-CHECK-INFER-JUDGE
💡 Exam Tips
💡 LOOK-CHECK-INFER-JUDGE: Look → Check (WACA) → Infer → Judge — use on EVERY source question.
💡 8-mark source: WACA + specific quotes + own knowledge. ALL THREE required.
💡 Cuba: Kennedy chose BLOCKADE over air strike — know WHY this is the key 16-mark judgement.
📝 Practice Exam Questions
Explain (6 marks)
Explain why the Berlin Blockade (June 1948–May 1949) was a significant turning point in the Cold War. Use specific evidence.
Source (8 marks) — Iron Curtain Speech
How useful is Churchill's Iron Curtain Speech for understanding the origins of the Cold War? Para 1: content + 2 direct quotes. Para 2: WACA provenance (note Truman's presence). Para 3: own knowledge.
Essay (16 marks)
'The USA was more responsible than the USSR for Cold War tensions.' Para 1 (USA) → Para 2 (USSR) → Para 3 (compare) → Conclusion.
🧠

Guides you · Socratic questioning · Essay & source skills
Hello! I am Socrates, your historical AI tutor. I am here to guide you through this topic with questions — I will never just give you the answer. What are you working on today?

Blog Spot — Revision
Make a claim · Support it with evidence · Respond like a historian
Was the USA or the USSR more responsible for the tensions that led to the Cold War?

Stalin's Russia 1924–53

📅 Key Dates
1924–29Stalin consolidates power — outmanoeuvres Trotsky
1928First Five-Year Plan launched
1932–33Holodomor — 3.5–7.5 million die in Ukraine
1936–38Great Terror — Show Trials, mass arrests, Gulag expands
📖 Key Terms
Power StruggleFive-Year PlanCollectivisationKulaksHolodomorGreat TerrorShow TrialsNKVDGulagCult of PersonalityPropaganda
💡 Exam Tips
💡 Cover BOTH terror (NKVD, Gulag) AND propaganda (cult, censorship) — both required for full marks.
💡 Apply LOOK-CHECK-INFER-JUDGE to Stalin's official portrait: what visual choices communicate power?
💡 Show Trials: explain WHY defendants confessed — torture, family threats, propaganda.
📝 Practice Exam Questions
Explain (6 marks)
Explain how Stalin maintained power through propaganda. Use specific evidence: cult of personality, censorship, Stakhanovites, and the role of the official portrait.
Source (8 marks) — Stalin's Portrait
How useful is Stalin's official portrait for understanding how Stalin maintained power? Para 1: LOOK. Para 2: CHECK (WACA). Para 3: own knowledge about what the portrait DOESN'T show.
Essay (16 marks)
'Terror was more important than propaganda in maintaining Stalin's control.' Para 1 (terror) → Para 2 (propaganda) → Para 3 (compare) → Conclusion.
🧠

Was terror more important than propaganda in maintaining Stalin's power?

Vietnam War 1954–75

📅 Key Dates
May 1954Dien Bien Phu — France defeated
Aug 1964Gulf of Tonkin — US escalation
Jan 1968Tet Offensive — 100+ cities attacked
30 Apr 1975Fall of Saigon
📖 Key Terms
Viet MinhVietcongHo Chi MinhDomino TheoryGulf of TonkinSearch and DestroyCu Chi TunnelsTet OffensiveMy LaiVietnamisation
💡 Exam Tips
💡 TET OFFENSIVE: military = US WON. Political = US public confidence shattered. BOTH parts needed.
💡 Why did USA fail? MULTIPLE factors — Vietcong tactics, US tactics, home front, nationalism.
💡 16-mark: 'How far was US military tactics the main reason?' — always challenge with alternatives.
📝 Practice Exam Questions
Explain (6 marks)
Explain why the Vietcong's tactics were so effective against US forces. Use specific evidence: Cu Chi tunnels, hit-and-run raids, booby traps, and blending with civilians.
Source (8 marks) — Vietnam Composite
How useful is the Vietnam War composite image for understanding why the USA failed? Para 1: LOOK. Para 2: CHECK. Para 3: own knowledge.
Essay (16 marks)
'The Tet Offensive was the most important reason why the USA failed in Vietnam.' Para 1 (Tet: military vs political) → Para 2 (other factors) → Para 3 (compare) → Conclusion.
🧠

Was the Tet Offensive the turning point of the Vietnam War — or just the moment the truth became visible?

Changes in Medicine c.1848–1948

📅 Key Dates
1928Fleming discovers penicillin — BEFORE WW2
1940Florey and Chain develop usable antibiotic — 12-year gap matters
Nov 1942Beveridge Report — Five Giants identified
5 Jul 1948NHS launches
📖 Key Terms
PenicillinFlemingFloreyChainBlood PlasmaGuinea Pig ClubBeveridge ReportWantDiseaseIgnoranceSqualorIdlenessNHSNye BevanBMA
💡 Exam Tips
💡 Apply LOOK-CHECK-INFER-JUDGE to the penicillin image — the 12-year gap between discovery (1928) and antibiotic (1940) is KEY.
💡 BMA opposed NHS — Bevan overcame them by giving consultants private patient rights.
💡 Name ALL FIVE Giants exactly: Want, Disease, Ignorance, Squalor, Idleness.
📝 Practice Exam Questions
Explain (6 marks)
Explain the significance of penicillin in the history of medicine. Use specific evidence: Fleming's 1928 discovery, Florey and Chain's 1940 development, and its use in WW2. Note the 12-year gap.
Source (8 marks) — Bevan's Speech
How useful is Bevan's 1948 NHS launch speech for understanding the creation of the NHS? Para 1: content + 2 direct quotes. Para 2: WACA provenance. Para 3: own knowledge.
Essay (16 marks)
'WW2 was the main reason the NHS was created in 1948.' Para 1 (WW2 advances) → Para 2 (other factors: Beveridge, Labour 1945) → Para 3 (compare) → Conclusion.
🧠

🔍 Source Studies — Primary Source Analysis
