📅 Year 7 — Medieval England
Commissioned by Bishop Odo of Bayeux
Y7
Bayeux Tapestry, c.1077
[VISUAL SOURCE] A 70-metre embroidered cloth depicting the Norman Conquest. Commissioned by Bishop O...
Written approximately 34 years after Hastings by monks
Y7
Anglo-Saxon Chronicle, c.1100
Harold was informed that William the Bastard had landed at Pevensey. Harold at once marched from the...
Visual source — 145 years AFTER the Black Death
Y7
Danse Macabre, Wolgemut, c.1493
[VISUAL SOURCE] Death taking victims from every social class. Made 145 years after the Black Death o...
⚓ Year 8 — Slavery, Industry & Empire
Published by abolitionists
Y8
Brookes Slave Ship Diagram, 1787
[VISUAL SOURCE] Cross-section diagram showing 482 enslaved people in rows. Spaces just 50-55cm high ...
🌍 Year 9 — World Wars & International Relations
Censored letter — military officers read all soldiers' post
Y9
British Soldier, Western Front, 1916 (IWM)
We are now in the third week at the front. The mud is indescribable. Men have been pulled under by i...
Ethiopia's appeal — Selassie was in exile after Italy's invasion
Y9
Haile Selassie, League of Nations, June 1936
I ask the 52 nations who have given the Ethiopian people a promise to help them: What have you done ...
🎯 iGCSE — Years 10 & 11
Truman sat on the platform — Churchill was no longer Prime Minister
iGCSE
Winston Churchill, Fulton, Missouri, 5 March 1946
From Stettin in the Baltic to Trieste in the Adriatic, an iron curtain has descended across the Cont...
U-2 spy plane photograph — presented to Kennedy and ExComm
iGCSE
CIA Aerial Photograph, Cuba, 1 November 1962
[VISUAL SOURCE] Aerial photo of Soviet missile sites in Cuba. Without analysts' labels, structures a...
Produced during the Great Terror — displayed throughout the USSR
iGCSE
Official Soviet Portrait, c.1930s (state commission)
[VISUAL SOURCE] Stalin in plain military uniform, calm and authoritative. Gaze directed slightly upw...
Multiple US government photographs — composite image
iGCSE
Vietnam War Composite Image, c.1965–1975
[VISUAL SOURCE] Multiple photographs assembled showing US military helicopters, Vietnamese fighters,...
Shows journey from Fleming's 1928 discovery to WW2-era mass production. KEY: BEFORE WW2.
iGCSE
Penicillin Educational Image
[VISUAL SOURCE] Penicillin's journey: from Fleming's 1928 petri dish discovery through Florey and Ch...
⚓ Year 8 — Industrial Britain & Empire
⚓ Year 8 · Farms to Factories
George Weerth on Bradford, 1845
"We look in astonishment... suddenly it is evening on a bright day..."
⚓ Year 8 · Victorian Living Conditions
Chadwick: Sanitary Report, 1842
"The annual loss of life from filth is greater than loss from wounds in war..."
🌍 Year 9 — World War One & The League
🌍 Year 9 · World War One
A Soldier's Letter from the Trenches, 1916
"The mud here is something beyond description. Men have drowned in it...."
🌍 Year 9 · League of Nations
Haile Selassie, Speech to the League, 1936
"It is us today. It will be you tomorrow...."
Y7
Bayeux Tapestry, c.1077
🖼️  VIEW this source image on Wikipedia Commons (opens in new tab)
📍 Source: Commissioned by Bishop Odo of Bayeux
[VISUAL SOURCE] A continuous narrative in 58 scenes: Harold's oath, death of Edward, Harold's coronation, William's invasion fleet, the Battle of Hastings, Harold's death, and William's victory.
📊 WACA Framework
WWho?
AAudience?
CContext?
AAim?
📝 Investigation Questions
WACA Question 1
Who commissioned this tapestry — and why does knowing this change how we use it?
Consider: Commissioned by Bishop Odo — William's half-brother. Would Odo commission a tapestry showing the Normans negatively?
WACA Question 2
When was it made — and who actually made it?
Consider: Made in the 1070s, probably by Anglo-Saxon embroiderers working for a Norman patron. What does it mean that the MAKERS are different from the COMMISSIONER?
WACA Question 3
What is this source most useful for — and what can it NOT tell us?
Consider: The Tapestry shows clothing, weapons, ships and battle tactics. Does it show Harold's perspective fairly?
🎯 Activities
🎯 Activity: Full WACA Analysis
  • Apply WACA fully: Who commissioned it? For what audience? In what context? With what aim?
  • Write your JUDGEMENT: 'This source is useful for understanding [X] because..., however it is limited because...'
🧠
Socrates, your historical AI tutor
Guides you · Socratic questioning · Essay & source skills
Hello! I am Socrates, your historical AI tutor. I am here to guide you through this topic with questions — I will never just give you the answer. What are you working on today?
💬
Blog Spot — Source Studies
Make a claim · Support it with evidence · Respond like a historian
Which historical argument from this topic is most convincing — and why?
Y7
Anglo-Saxon Chronicle, c.1100
🖼️  VIEW this source image on Wikipedia Commons (opens in new tab)
📍 Source: Written approximately 34 years after Hastings by monks
Harold was informed that William the Bastard had landed at Pevensey. Harold at once marched from the north. Many men fell ill on the march and many remained behind. William came by surprise against him before his army was set in order.
📊 WACA Framework
WWho?
AAudience?
CContext?
AAim?
📝 Investigation Questions
WACA Question 1
Who wrote this source and how might their identity affect what they recorded?
Consider: Written by monks — educated men associated with the Church. Would monks be sympathetic to Harold (Anglo-Saxon) or William (Norman)?
WACA Question 2
When was this written — and why does the 34-year gap matter?
Consider: Written approximately 34 years after 1066. What might monks writing in 1100 know or not know?
WACA Question 3
What is this source most useful for — and what are its specific limitations?
Consider: What does this source tell us clearly? What does it NOT tell us?
🎯 Activities
🎯 Activity: 8-Mark Practice
  • Question: 'How useful is the Anglo-Saxon Chronicle for understanding why Harold lost at Hastings?'
  • Para 1: content + 2 direct quotes. Para 2: WACA provenance. Para 3: own knowledge. Target 150-200 words.
🧠
Socrates, your historical AI tutor
Guides you · Socratic questioning · Essay & source skills
Hello! I am Socrates, your historical AI tutor. I am here to guide you through this topic with questions — I will never just give you the answer. What are you working on today?
💬
Blog Spot — Source Studies
Make a claim · Support it with evidence · Respond like a historian
Which historical argument from this topic is most convincing — and why?
Y7
Danse Macabre, Wolgemut, c.1493
🖼️  VIEW this source image on Wikipedia Commons (opens in new tab)
📍 Source: Visual source — 145 years AFTER the Black Death
[VISUAL SOURCE] Skeletal figures of Death dancing with people from every social class — popes, kings, merchants, knights and peasants — all being led to their graves equally. No social rank escapes Death's summons.
📊 WACA Framework
WWho?
AAudience?
CContext?
AAim?
📝 Investigation Questions
WACA Question 1
What specific visual choices has the artist made — and what message do they send?
Consider: Death takes everyone equally regardless of social rank. What does this tell us about how people in 1493 understood the Black Death?
WACA Question 2
When was this made — and why does the 145-year gap matter?
Consider: Made in 1493 — 145 years after the Black Death of 1348. The artist did NOT witness the plague.
WACA Question 3
How does this visual source complement John of Reading's written account?
Consider: John of Reading described symptoms. The Danse Macabre shows the EMOTIONAL response.
🎯 Activities
🎯 Activity: LOOK-CHECK-INFER-JUDGE
  • LOOK: List FIVE specific visual elements
  • CHECK (WACA): Who? When (1493 — 145 years after)? For whom? What aim?
  • JUDGE: How useful for understanding the Black Death of 1348? 150-200 words.
🧠
Socrates, your historical AI tutor
Guides you · Socratic questioning · Essay & source skills
Hello! I am Socrates, your historical AI tutor. I am here to guide you through this topic with questions — I will never just give you the answer. What are you working on today?
💬
Blog Spot — Source Studies
Make a claim · Support it with evidence · Respond like a historian
Which historical argument from this topic is most convincing — and why?
Y8
Brookes Slave Ship Diagram, 1787
🖼️  VIEW this source image on Wikipedia Commons (opens in new tab)
📍 Source: Published by abolitionists
[VISUAL SOURCE] Cross-section diagram of the Brookes' lower decks, showing 482 enslaved people laid out in rows. Measurements show the height of spaces (50-55cm) and width per person (16 inches).
📊 WACA Framework
WWho?
AAudience?
CContext?
AAim?
📝 Investigation Questions
WACA Question 1
Who produced this — and what was its specific aim?
Consider: Produced by ABOLITIONISTS. Does knowing it is propaganda make it less useful? Or more useful as evidence of abolitionist methods?
WACA Question 2
What is this diagram designed to make viewers FEEL — and how does it achieve this?
Consider: The diagram uses precise measurements. Why use a TECHNICAL diagram rather than a painting?
WACA Question 3
How does this visual source complement Equiano's written account?
Consider: Equiano described what the Middle Passage FELT like. The Brookes shows what it looked like from OUTSIDE.
🎯 Activities
🎯 Activity: LOOK-CHECK-INFER-JUDGE
  • LOOK: Describe what you can see — layout, measurements, number of figures, labels
  • CHECK (WACA): Who? When? For whom? What abolitionist aim?
  • JUDGE: Write your judgement sentence. 150-200 words.
🧠
Socrates, your historical AI tutor
Guides you · Socratic questioning · Essay & source skills
Hello! I am Socrates, your historical AI tutor. I am here to guide you through this topic with questions — I will never just give you the answer. What are you working on today?
💬
Blog Spot — Source Studies
Make a claim · Support it with evidence · Respond like a historian
Which historical argument from this topic is most convincing — and why?
Y9
British Soldier, Western Front, 1916 (IWM)
🖼️  VIEW this source image on Wikipedia Commons (opens in new tab)
📍 Source: Censored letter — military officers read all soldiers' post
We are now in the third week at the front. The mud is indescribable. Men have been pulled under by it entirely. Last night we lost two men who disappeared into shell holes. The noise never stops. I have not slept properly in eleven days. I think about home every moment.
📊 WACA Framework
WWho?
AAudience?
CContext?
AAim?
📝 Investigation Questions
WACA Question 1
Who wrote this — and why does the CENSORSHIP of the letter matter?
Consider: The letter was censored — meaning the soldier knew it would be read by a military officer. How might this affect what he chose to write?
WACA Question 2
What does this source tell us — and what can it NOT tell us?
Consider: Excellent for showing individual human experience. But can it tell us about strategic decisions?
WACA Question 3
Compare with the trench photographs in your WW1 topic section.
Consider: The photographs show the physical environment; the letter shows the psychological experience.
🎯 Activities
🎯 Activity: WACA — The Problem of Censorship
  • Apply WACA fully — particularly CONTEXT: who censored this letter? Why? What might be deliberately missing?
  • 8-mark practice: 'How useful is this soldier's letter for understanding the human cost of WW1?' 150-200 words.
🧠
Socrates, your historical AI tutor
Guides you · Socratic questioning · Essay & source skills
Hello! I am Socrates, your historical AI tutor. I am here to guide you through this topic with questions — I will never just give you the answer. What are you working on today?
💬
Blog Spot — Source Studies
Make a claim · Support it with evidence · Respond like a historian
Which historical argument from this topic is most convincing — and why?
Y9
Haile Selassie, League of Nations, June 1936
🖼️  VIEW this source image on Wikipedia Commons (opens in new tab)
📍 Source: Ethiopia's appeal — Selassie was in exile after Italy's invasion
I ask the 52 nations who have given the Ethiopian people a promise to help them: What have you done for Ethiopia? God and history will remember your judgement. It is not merely a question of the settlement of Italian aggression. It is collective security: it is the very existence of the League of Nations.
📊 WACA Framework
WWho?
AAudience?
CContext?
AAim?
📝 Investigation Questions
WACA Question 1
Who is speaking, to whom — and why does the OCCASION matter?
Consider: Selassie had to travel to Geneva personally — his country had been conquered and he was in exile.
WACA Question 2
What specific arguments does Selassie make — and how does he make them persuasive?
Consider: He invokes 'God and history.' He frames it as 'collective security' — not just Ethiopia.
WACA Question 3
How useful is this source for understanding why the League of Nations failed?
Consider: Made AFTER the League had already failed. What does this speech itself tell us about the League's structural weaknesses?
🎯 Activities
🎯 Activity: WACA + Compare with Photograph
  • Apply WACA to this speech fully. Then: how does looking at the photograph change how you understand his words?
  • 8-mark practice: 'How useful is Haile Selassie's 1936 speech for understanding why the League failed?' 150-200 words.
🧠
Socrates, your historical AI tutor
Guides you · Socratic questioning · Essay & source skills
Hello! I am Socrates, your historical AI tutor. I am here to guide you through this topic with questions — I will never just give you the answer. What are you working on today?
💬
Blog Spot — Source Studies
Make a claim · Support it with evidence · Respond like a historian
Which historical argument from this topic is most convincing — and why?
iGCSE
Winston Churchill, Fulton, Missouri, 5 March 1946
🖼️  VIEW this source image on Wikipedia Commons (opens in new tab)
📍 Source: Truman sat on the platform — Churchill was no longer Prime Minister
From Stettin in the Baltic to Trieste in the Adriatic, an iron curtain has descended across the Continent. Behind that line lie all the capitals of Central and Eastern Europe — Warsaw, Berlin, Prague, Vienna, Budapest, Belgrade, Bucharest and Sofia.
📊 WACA Framework
WWho?
AAudience?
CContext?
AAim?
📝 Investigation Questions
WACA Question 1
Who gave this speech — and why does Truman's presence on the platform matter?
Consider: Churchill was no longer PM — a private citizen. Yet Truman sat beside him. Does Truman's presence give Churchill's words US government backing?
WACA Question 2
What impression does Churchill give — and how does his language achieve this?
Consider: The phrase 'iron curtain' — why such effective political language? He lists specific European capitals — why name them all?
WACA Question 3
How useful is this source for understanding the origins of the Cold War?
Consider: This speech is both a CAUSE of the Cold War and a DESCRIPTION of it. Can a source be both simultaneously?
🎯 Activities
🎯 Activity: LOOK-CHECK-INFER-JUDGE
  • LOOK: What specific claims does Churchill make?
  • CHECK (WACA): Who? Where/when? Audience (Truman's presence)? Aim?
  • 8-mark practice: 'How useful is Churchill's Iron Curtain speech for understanding why the Cold War began?' 150-200 words.
🧠
Socrates, your historical AI tutor
Guides you · Socratic questioning · Essay & source skills
Hello! I am Socrates, your historical AI tutor. I am here to guide you through this topic with questions — I will never just give you the answer. What are you working on today?
💬
Blog Spot — Source Studies
Make a claim · Support it with evidence · Respond like a historian
Which historical argument from this topic is most convincing — and why?
iGCSE
CIA Aerial Photograph, Cuba, 1 November 1962
🖼️  VIEW this source image on Wikipedia Commons (opens in new tab)
📍 Source: U-2 spy plane photograph — presented to Kennedy and ExComm
[VISUAL SOURCE] An aerial photograph showing a cleared area of Cuban jungle from directly above. Analysts' annotations label specific structures: LAUNCH POSITION, ERECTOR, MISSILE READY TENT, MISSILE TRANSPORTERS. Without these labels, the structures are difficult to identify.
📊 WACA Framework
WWho?
AAudience?
CContext?
AAim?
📝 Investigation Questions
WACA Question 1
Who took this — and why does the CIA source fundamentally affect how we use it?
Consider: This was taken by the CIA during direct confrontation with the USSR. Does a photograph simply record what was there regardless of who took it?
WACA Question 2
What can this photograph show — and what can it NOT show?
Consider: The photograph shows structures. But does it show Soviet INTENTIONS? Who authorised the missiles?
WACA Question 3
How does this photographic evidence relate to Churchill's Iron Curtain speech (1946)?
Consider: Churchill described a Soviet sphere of control in 1946. By 1962, Soviet missiles in Cuba represented a direct military threat.
🎯 Activities
🎯 Activity: LOOK-CHECK-INFER-JUDGE
  • LOOK: Without the labels, what can you actually see? What does this tell us about the role of EXPERT INTERPRETATION?
  • CHECK (WACA): Who took this? When? For whom? CIA's aim?
  • 8-mark practice: 'How useful is this CIA aerial photograph for understanding the Cuban Missile Crisis?' 150-200 words.
🧠
Socrates, your historical AI tutor
Guides you · Socratic questioning · Essay & source skills
Hello! I am Socrates, your historical AI tutor. I am here to guide you through this topic with questions — I will never just give you the answer. What are you working on today?
💬
Blog Spot — Source Studies
Make a claim · Support it with evidence · Respond like a historian
Which historical argument from this topic is most convincing — and why?
iGCSE
Official Soviet Portrait, c.1930s (state commission)
🖼️  VIEW this source image on Wikipedia Commons (opens in new tab)
📍 Source: Produced during the Great Terror — displayed throughout the USSR
[VISUAL SOURCE] The portrait shows Stalin in a plain military uniform, with a calm and authoritative expression. He appears middle-aged, strong and composed. His gaze is directed slightly upward. There are no other people, no weapons, no signs of violence or terror.
📊 WACA Framework
WWho?
AAudience?
CContext?
AAim?
📝 Investigation Questions
WACA Question 1
LOOK: What specific visual choices has the artist made — and what do they communicate?
Consider: List at least FIVE specific visual elements — clothing, expression, pose, lighting, gaze direction, background.
WACA Question 2
CHECK: Who produced this image — and for what audience and purpose?
Consider: This was a STATE COMMISSION. Who was the intended audience? What was the aim? Note: made DURING the Great Terror.
WACA Question 3
JUDGE: How useful is this portrait — and what does the GAP with historical reality tell us?
Consider: This portrait shows none of the Gulag, none of the Holodomor, none of the Show Trials.
🎯 Activities
🎯 Activity: Full LOOK-CHECK-INFER-JUDGE
  • Apply LOOK → CHECK → INFER → JUDGE
  • Write your JUDGEMENT: 'This portrait is useful for understanding [specifically what] because..., however the gap between this image and historical reality tells us...'
  • 8-mark practice: 'How useful is this official Soviet portrait for understanding how Stalin maintained power?' 150-200 words.
🧠
Socrates, your historical AI tutor
Guides you · Socratic questioning · Essay & source skills
Hello! I am Socrates, your historical AI tutor. I am here to guide you through this topic with questions — I will never just give you the answer. What are you working on today?
💬
Blog Spot — Source Studies
Make a claim · Support it with evidence · Respond like a historian
Which historical argument from this topic is most convincing — and why?
iGCSE
Vietnam War Composite Image, c.1965–1975
🖼️  VIEW this source image on Wikipedia Commons (opens in new tab)
📍 Source: Multiple US government photographs — composite image
[VISUAL SOURCE] Multiple photographs assembled into one image: US military power, Vietnamese resistance, civilian suffering, and anti-war protests. This composite illustrates the war's complexity and contested nature.
📊 WACA Framework
WWho?
AAudience?
CContext?
AAim?
📝 Investigation Questions
WACA Question 1
What are the advantages and limitations of using a COMPOSITE image?
Consider: A composite combines multiple photographs. What does this allow us to see? But what is LOST when photographs are assembled?
WACA Question 2
What does each element suggest about WHY the USA failed in Vietnam?
Consider: US military → firepower? Vietnamese fighters → guerrilla warfare? Civilians → alienation?
WACA Question 3
How does this compare with your Tet Offensive PPT?
Consider: Your Tet PPT contains factual information. This composite provides visual impressions.
🎯 Activities
🎯 Activity: LOOK-CHECK-INFER-JUDGE
  • LOOK: Identify each visual element and what it represents
  • CHECK: Who produced this composite? For whom? What is the PURPOSE of presenting multiple images?
  • 8-mark practice: 'How useful is this composite for understanding why the USA failed in Vietnam?' 150-200 words.
🧠
Socrates, your historical AI tutor
Guides you · Socratic questioning · Essay & source skills
Hello! I am Socrates, your historical AI tutor. I am here to guide you through this topic with questions — I will never just give you the answer. What are you working on today?
💬
Blog Spot — Source Studies
Make a claim · Support it with evidence · Respond like a historian
Which historical argument from this topic is most convincing — and why?
iGCSE
Penicillin Educational Image
🖼️  VIEW this source image on Wikipedia Commons (opens in new tab)
📍 Source: Shows journey from Fleming's 1928 discovery to WW2-era mass production. KEY: BEFORE WW2.
[VISUAL SOURCE] Three stages shown: Fleming's 1928 petri dish discovery; Florey and Chain's 1940 laboratory work; WW2-era industrial-scale mass production. NOTE: Fleming discovered penicillin in 1928 — BEFORE WW2.
📊 WACA Framework
WWho?
AAudience?
CContext?
AAim?
📝 Investigation Questions
WACA Question 1
LOOK-CHECK-INFER-JUDGE: What does each stage in the image represent?
Consider: Fleming discovered penicillin by ACCIDENT in 1928. Florey and Chain spent 12 years developing it. WW2 drove MASS PRODUCTION.
WACA Question 2
Why was there a 12-YEAR GAP between Fleming's discovery (1928) and its use as medicine (1940)?
Consider: How does this gap affect the argument that 'WW2 caused the development of penicillin'? Was WW2 a CAUSE or just an ACCELERATOR?
WACA Question 3
How useful is this image for understanding the relationship between WW2 and the NHS?
Consider: The NHS was created in 1948. But Fleming discovered penicillin in 1928 — before WW2.
🎯 Activities
🎯 Activity: LOOK-CHECK-INFER-JUDGE + 16-Mark Plan
  • LOOK: Identify each stage shown — discovery, development, mass production
  • CHECK (WACA): Who produced this educational image? For whom? What aim?
  • 16-mark plan: 'How far did WW2 cause the creation of the NHS in 1948?' → Para 1 → Para 2 → Para 3 → Conclusion.
🧠
Socrates, your historical AI tutor
Guides you · Socratic questioning · Essay & source skills
Hello! I am Socrates, your historical AI tutor. I am here to guide you through this topic with questions — I will never just give you the answer. What are you working on today?
💬
